speaker: interviewer
text: August 10, 1994, Dana Schwartz, tape five. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: OK [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] We-- we were in Bergen-Belsen for quite a while. And then, they started sending in a lot of prisoners from different camps. And we heard all kind of rumors that possibly Germany's losing the war, and they're [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sending the people all to Bergen-Belsen. It's going to be terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the first chance we had, we volunteered to be sent out to a different camp. And again, we were shipped in-- in cattle cars, no stockings, no socks, no shoes in the middle of the winter, and they dragged us for seven days in the train. When we got off the train at our destination, which was a factory where they were making parts to planes and tanks, the Germans that came to pick us up said to the Germans who brought us, they asked them soldiers, did you give the women vodka that they're so drunk that they can't walk? Because we couldn't walk because we had no water-- for, I think, seven days. Lips were swollen. It was just terrible. So when they took us off and we started walking toward the camp, we came across farms. And they-- they had these water things for pigs, those wooden-- Troughs.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. What do you call them?

speaker: interviewer
text: Troughs.

speaker: interviewee
text: Troughs-- and we just run to the troughs. And we tried to drink that water. And the Germans started throwing-- the farmer started throwing stones at us and wouldn't let us have the-- the water. But we got to the camp, and we were all very sick. We had frozen-- we had-- our legs were frozen, and our hands were frozen. It was just-- we were just in a mess. So we couldn't work. And the German in charge of the factory, later on, we found out that he bought us. He actually bought us. They actually-- the people that were working in factories were bought and sold like slaves. And we found that out because he brought a doctor in to look at us. And the doctor said to him, these people won't be able to work. And he said, listen, they cost me a lot of money. They better work. So he bought us. We found out later on that he actually bought us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were actually a slave.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And-- also, he brought in a couple doctors, and they all said no, no, no. They-- these people will never walk again. But they brought in a-- a-- like a-- again, like a-- a country doctor, who brought with him big jars but-- not jars-- those big urns with some kind of salve. And we had to put it on our feet and wrap it around. They gave us torn blankets. Anyhow, we-- it went away. I wish I had that recipe today for that salve. And we were liberated in that camp by the Americans, who shortly after had to leave that because the Russian claimed that they did all the fighting. But there was a awful lot of bridges there that were bombed. So they claimed that they couldn't come into the towns fast enough, that the American soldiers came with parachutes, but they did the fighting, the-- so the-- the American soldiers had to leave that part of Germany, and the Russians occupied it. But before they left, they gave us a proposition. We could go with them to the American zone or stay with the Russians. Of course, we all went with the Americans, but that's a different story. I think I wanted to tell you the story about the Red Cross. While we were in the factory, some of the Germans who were in-- in charge started to complain that they get very little food. So they found out, somehow or other, that the commandant of the camp, instead of bringing the food-- now, I'm talking about the Germans, not just us-- into the camp, that he was selling them-- the food on the black market.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it so happened that at that time I worked in the German kitchen. So I knew that the food wasn't coming in like it was supposed to be coming in, too. And they started to complain terribly that they weren't getting their rations of food. And they found out why. So when they found out why, they came to us, to our head of the group, and they said, look, the only way we can stop him from stealing your food and our food is for us to write to the Red-- International Red Cross, but we want to have your signatures. So we were afraid of them, you know? If they wanted something, we give it to them. So we said, fine. We'll give you the signatures. So they got the signatures, and they sent the letter to the Red Cross.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, they told us prior to the time that the Red Cross was coming in, they told us that tomorrow, the Red Cross is gonna come in, and they're gonna ask you about the food you're getting, how much, what kind of food. Tell them the truth because you're not getting the full ration. So and they said, we do the same thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had a kind of meeting after work. And we said, what we gonna do? I mean, the commandant of the camp is gonna be there, and some of the Germans that are not in on this little story. They're gonna kill us if we complain to the Red Cross. So we had over there in the factory, we had a-- a bathroom, which had 13 seats. It was a wooden-- a typical European type of a bathroom. You must know what I'm talking about with the holes. So every time we complained to the commandment of the camp about something, the work was too hard or too late, or this or that, he always used to say, what do you have to complain about? You have 13 holes. That was his answer to everything. So that evening we had decided that when they ask us something, if they ask you, or me, or somebody else, the answer should be, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Now, they're not going to be stupid. They're going to realize that if everyone gives the same answer that something is not kosher here. Well, the next day, they put us in a circle, the representatives of the Red Cross came. There were three men and two women. And they went in the middle of the circle, and they would point to one woman. They would say, tell me, are you getting enough food? And her answer was, [SPEAKING GERMAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: And then she would ask somebody else. And they would say, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. And this went on for about a half hour. And they walked away with the commandant laughing, joking, and they just completely ignored us after that.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was no investigation. Nobody came in and say, why did you do that? It was like even if we told them the truth, they wouldn't care-- they couldn't care less. And that was the experience with the Red Cross. That's why I have absolutely no-- no respect for them. I don't care what they tell me, how much they do for people. For us, they didn't do a god damned thing. If anything, they seemed to us just as bad as the Nazis. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Tell me, what happened to you after you were liberated? Did you go to look for anyone?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the beginning, no, because we didn't believe that-- each group will tell you the same thing. That wherever you were liberated, you wouldn't think that there was any people left. After what we saw, what we went through, you just-- you were positive that there was no one left but you, or your group, immediate group.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find anyone finally?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I found-- I found-- all-- we-- all the children survived, even though we were all separated. And after the war, we found each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father was killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was killed. So it was the five children. We all survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you stay together?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed together. From that-- from the moment that we met, we stayed together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you-- did you all come here?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not all. I was the first one with my brother, my younger brother, to come to the States.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you had been in a DP camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in a DP camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in South Simon Frankfurt, Frankfurt [? Demine ?]. So from there I had no family. I had nobody to bring me to the United States. And Mrs. Eleanore Roosevelt brought over 500 children that she supposedly adopted. There was no legal adoption but she guaranteed that she'll take care of us, which she did. So she brought 500 of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever meet her?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met her once. I met her when I refused to go because I changed my mind. I didn't want to go alone. And that's when she put my brother on my papers. She changed his age because he was older than she was allowed to adopt. He was 18 already. So she changed-- she had his age changed.

speaker: interviewer
text: To?

speaker: interviewee
text: To 16. And my brother, he-- he lives here, and he has three different ages. He's a very interesting character. He has his true age, he has the age that Mrs. Roosevelt had changed, and he has the age, that while he was in Poland he changed because he didn't want to go into the army after the war, into the Russian Army. So I-- we always joke, how old are you, really? Which is your true age?

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you when you got to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, I went to school for almost two years. And I went to a special school. We had a school where we were learning how to speak English at the same time we were learning the three R's. And I was in a foster home for a while. And then I went to live with friends. And eventually, I met my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had three children. I-- I-- I-- I had two children and one adopted. My husband was a widower with a child. So I raised the boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name, your husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Milton Gelfman.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you live together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, 33 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he got sick and passed.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remarried?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I would now like to invite your--

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband and my son. My-- be a pleasure. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Introduce your family to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Certainly, a pleasure. This is my husband, Jerry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hi.

speaker: interviewee
text: Say hello to Dana. My son, Ronnald, and my daughter-in-law, Joanne.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hello.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hi. How did your mother's going through the Holocaust change your life, or make your life different from your non-Holocaust survivors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my earliest memories of that were children when I was in say, first grade or so, would first of all, ask me how come my mom spoke so funny. And having grown up in the house and not having been aware of it, I looked at them and said, what do you mean funny? And then they brought up the fact that she had an accent, which to me, I never heard. Of course, now, I'm much more aware of it. But back then I had no basis of comparison. So I just assumed that all moms spoke like that. And she did, from about that time and perhaps a little bit younger-- I'd have a hard time placing that. But she did fill us in occasionally-- us, meaning myself, and my younger sister, and my older brother-- occasionally on some of the things that she had gone through. And of course, your hearing it after the fact and many years after the fact. And you don't really relate to it the same way you would as if you yourself had been through it. But you know, we were made aware of it at a very early age.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it-- did it affect your life very strongly at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a tough question. Personally, I'm-- I'm not a practicing Jew. I'm not an observant Jew. I have some [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] predilection to enjoy the-- the traditions, shall we say. But as far as feeling an affinity, for instance, to Israel or something like that, I-- I-- I-- in all honesty, I'd have to say no, it didn't really affect me all that much. But I am concerned. I pay perhaps much, much more attention than the average American Jew when I hear things like what happened in Argentina last week and what happened the day after in London. I-- I-- I-- my ears prick up a little bit, and I pay a little bit more attention, perhaps, but I wouldn't say I'm paranoid about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. Do you have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any grandchildren yet?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have one in New Jersey. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How old is he now?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's eight months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Boy. Yeah, my daughter's son.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she has five more up here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, right. We have five here. We have four boys and one girl here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wonderful, wonderful. Well, we're going to-- well, I'd like to show the photographs that you have of your remaining children. And I believe you have one photograph of your father from before the war, which we are very excited to see next. Thank you very much, Mary.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pleasure.

speaker: interviewee
text: Terrific.

speaker: interviewer
text: Great. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: The only picture I have of my dad is a picture from a cemetery when he was visiting the grave of a dear friend of his. And unfortunately, this is the only picture I have. And I got it in the United States from a friend of ours who brought it with him when he came here before the war. I wish I had some more, but that's it. No picture of mother, no picture of father. That's it. That's all the pictures. Sorry. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My dad was killed just a couple months before the war ended. I know it because my brother was with him. At the time when he was killed, he was in his early 40s. I don't know exactly how much. His name was Szaja Rybowski. He was quite well known in our city. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] this is my daughter, Shelly, and her husband, Howard. This is Mr. and Mrs. Howard Zorfiss. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The next picture I want to show you is my grandson that my daughter surprised me with. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this is my pride and joy. This is the only grandson I have from my daughter. I hope she'll have more. But right now this little guy is all we have, and he's some guy. I don't see him too much. He lives in New Jersey. But I love him very much. And his name is Andrew. I can't even pronounce it. I said, I'm gonna call him Drewy. It's much easier. That's it. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is my son, Ronnie, and his lovely wife, Joanna. I'm very proud of them. And I'm very glad that they live in Los Angeles. And we're very close, see each other very often. And unfortunately, I don't have a picture of my oldest son. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I do have them, but they're put away, and it would take some time to look for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this my family, my daughter, Shelly, her husband, Howard, my son, Ronald, and Joanna. Sorry, Stevie. Next time you send me a nice, big picture of yourself.
